I. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x0 if f(x) gets closer to L as x approaches x0.
II. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x0 if, for any ε > 0, there corresponds a δ > 0 such that  < ε whenever 0 <  < δ.
III. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x0 if, given any ε > 0, there exists a value of x for which  < ε.
questions.prompt_text: Solve the problem. Identify the incorrect statements about limits. I. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x 0 if f(x) gets closer to L as x approaches x 0 . II. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x 0 if, for any ε > 0, there corresponds a δ > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < ε whenever 0 < |x - (x) with subscript (0)| < δ. III. The number L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches x 0 if, given any ε > 0, there exists a value of x for which |f(x) - L| < ε.

questions.solution_html:
Let ε > 0. Since f(x) = L, there exists δ1 such that if 0 < |x - a| < δ1, then 
|f(x) - L| < . Since g(x) = K, there exists δ2 such that if 0 < |x - a| < δ2, then 
|g(x) - K| < . If 0 < |x - a| < δ = min{δ1, δ2}, then 
|(f(x) + g(x)) - (L + K)| ≤ |f(x) - L| + |g(x) - K| <  +  = ε. Thus (f(x) + g(x)) = L + K.
